stem: A point has polar coordinates \( \left(r,\theta\right)=\left([[r]],\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}\right) \). Find its rectangular \( x \)-coordinate.
distractor_rationales: This uses \( r\sin\theta\) (the \( y\)-formula) instead of \( r\cos\theta\).
This has the wrong sign for the quadrant containing \( \theta\).
This drops the numerator \( k\), using the base angle \( \pi/n\) instead of the full angle.
This forgets to multiply by \( r\) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( x\)-coordinate.

stem: A point has polar coordinates \( \left(r,\theta\right)=\left([[r]],\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{[[n]]}\right) \). Find its rectangular \( y \)-coordinate.
distractor_rationales: This uses \( r\cos\theta\) (the \( x\)-formula) instead of \( r\sin\theta\).
This has the wrong sign for the quadrant containing \( \theta\).
This drops the numerator \( k\), using the base angle \( \pi/n\) instead of the full angle.
This forgets to multiply by \( r\) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-coordinate.

stem: A point has rectangular coordinates \( ([[x]],[[y]]) \). Find its polar radial coordinate \( r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root, reporting \( r^{2}\) instead of \( r\).
This uses taxicab (Manhattan) distance instead of the true radial distance.
This subtracts the squared coordinates instead of adding them.
This takes the square root of each coordinate separately before adding, instead of adding first.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( r\).
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value of \( r\).

stem: A point has rectangular coordinates \( ([[x]],[[y]]) \), which lies in the first quadrant. Find its polar angle \( \theta \) (in radians), where \( 0<\theta<\dfrac{\pi}{2} \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, using \( x/y\) instead of \( y/x\).
This gives a negative angle rather than a first-quadrant angle.
This reports the complementary angle instead of \( \theta\) itself.
This reports the supplementary angle instead of \( \theta\) itself.
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct angle.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct angle.
